description: Synthetic clinical encounter transcripts for SmartScribe demo and testing. These are NOT real patient encounters.

transcripts.title: Follow-up Visit — Diabetes & Hypertension
transcripts.transcript:
Patient comes in for 3-month follow-up on diabetes and blood pressure. She says the metformin is working okay but she gets some stomach upset after taking it, especially if she doesn't eat first. She's been checking her sugars at home, mostly running 130-160 fasting. She ran out of her lisinopril about a week ago and hasn't been able to get it refilled. She denies any chest pain, shortness of breath, or swelling in her legs. She says she's been trying to walk more but it's hard with her knee pain. She's been eating better, cutting back on sodas. No vision changes. No numbness or tingling in her feet.

Vitals today: BP 148/92, HR 78, Temp 98.4, Weight 198 lbs (down 3 lbs from last visit). A1C came back at 7.8%, up from 7.4% three months ago. BMP is normal, creatinine 0.9. Foot exam: pulses intact, sensation intact to monofilament bilaterally. No skin breakdown.

Assessment: Type 2 diabetes not at goal with A1C trending up. Hypertension uncontrolled — likely related to medication lapse. Knee osteoarthritis limiting exercise.

Plan: Continue metformin 1000mg BID, counsel to take with food. Refill lisinopril 20mg daily, stress importance of not missing doses. Recheck A1C in 3 months. If still above 7.5%, will consider adding a GLP-1 agonist. Refer to PT for knee. Discussed diet and exercise goals. Follow up in 3 months.
transcripts.demo_scenario: Classic primary care follow-up — great for showing the SOAP note transformation

transcripts.title: New Patient — Persistent Headaches
transcripts.transcript:
New patient, 34 year old male, presenting with headaches for about 2 months. He describes them as a dull pressure on both sides of his head, worse in the afternoons, rates them 5-6/10. No aura, no nausea or vomiting. He works at a computer all day and thinks it might be related to screen time. He's been taking ibuprofen 400mg 3-4 times a week with some relief. No history of migraines in the family. He does drink about 4 cups of coffee a day. Sleep is okay, about 6 hours a night. No recent head trauma. No vision changes, no weakness, no numbness.

Exam: Alert and oriented. HEENT normal, no sinus tenderness. Neck: no stiffness, mild tension in bilateral trapezius muscles. Neuro exam grossly normal — cranial nerves intact, strength 5/5 throughout, reflexes 2+ and symmetric. Fundoscopic exam normal.

Thinking this is tension-type headache, likely related to ergonomics and possibly caffeine and sleep. Not concerned about secondary causes given normal neuro exam and no red flags.

Plan: Trial of daily stretching exercises and ergonomic workstation adjustment. Reduce caffeine gradually to 1-2 cups. Aim for 7-8 hours sleep. Can continue ibuprofen PRN but limit to 2x/week to avoid rebound. If no improvement in 4-6 weeks, will consider amitriptyline 10mg at bedtime for prevention. Return in 6 weeks or sooner if worsening.
transcripts.demo_scenario: New patient visit — shows well-structured H&P generation

transcripts.title: Urgent Visit — Shortness of Breath (Cardiology)
transcripts.transcript:
patient with known CHF EF 35% last echo 6 months ago, on carvedilol 25mg BID, lisinopril 40mg, furosemide 40mg daily, and spironolactone 25mg. Presents with 3 days of worsening dyspnea on exertion, now getting SOB walking from bedroom to bathroom. Also noticed increased LE edema and 5 lb weight gain over the past week. He admits he ran out of his furosemide 4 days ago. Has been eating more salt — holidays. Sleeping on 3 pillows (baseline is 2). Denies chest pain, palpitations, or syncope.

Vitals: BP 110/70, HR 92, RR 22, O2 sat 93% on RA. JVP elevated to 10cm. Lungs with bibasilar crackles. Heart RRR, S3 gallop present. 2+ pitting edema to mid-shin bilaterally. BNP 890 (baseline was 250). CXR shows bilateral pleural effusions and vascular congestion. BMP: Na 134, K 4.2, Cr 1.3 (baseline 1.1). Troponin negative.

Acute decompensated heart failure, likely precipitated by medication non-adherence and dietary indiscretion.

Restarted furosemide 80mg IV now, then 40mg IV q12h. Strict I&O, daily weights. Continue home meds. Restrict fluids to 1.5L/day, low sodium diet. Recheck BMP in AM. If good diuresis overnight and feeling better, may transition to oral and plan discharge tomorrow. If not improving, will increase diuretic or add metolazone. Discussed importance of medication adherence and salt restriction. Will arrange close follow-up in heart failure clinic within 1 week of discharge.
transcripts.demo_scenario: Cardiology acute visit — shows specialty-specific note generation with complex medical terminology
